summary: Graham argues that procrastination is inevitable, so the real question is how to procrastinate well by avoiding low-value errands in favor of meaningful work. The most impressive people are type-C procrastinators who delay small tasks to make room for big, ambitious projects that require long, uninterrupted stretches and the right mood.
key_points: Procrastination is unavoidable; the goal is to prioritize more important work over errands.
Great work often requires large, uninterrupted blocks of time and cannot be scheduled into small slices.
Type-B procrastination feels productive but is dangerous because it replaces big work with busywork.
Big problems are psychologically intimidating, so progress often requires indirect approaches and self-tricking.
Letting delight pull you toward ambitious work is more effective than pushing yourself with to-do lists.
money_quotes: So the question is not how to avoid procrastination, but how to procrastinate well.
Good procrastination is avoiding errands to do real work.
The most dangerous form of procrastination is unacknowledged type-B procrastination, because it doesn't feel like procrastination.
What's the best thing you could be working on, and why aren't you?
I think the way to "solve" the problem of procrastination is to let delight pull you instead of making a to-do list push you.
key_insight: Procrastination becomes productive when you deliberately ignore low-value errands to create the time and focus needed for ambitious, meaningful work.
